title: Cutting in and rolling a wall
subtitle: Cut the edges clean, roll the field, work wet-edge to wet-edge
excerpt: The technique for applying a smooth, consistent coat of emulsion to a wall: cutting in the perimeter with a brush before rolling, maintaining a wet edge to avoid lap marks, and checking coverage in raking light.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: Painting a clean, straight line with a brush at the edges of a wall (along the ceiling line, the cornice, the skirting, and around doors and windows) before filling the central area with a roller. The brushed strip prevents the roller from touching the adjacent surface.

content.text: Applying paint to a wall takes two operations in sequence:
cutting in

with a brush at the edges, then rolling the open field. The key constraint is the wet edge: if the cut-in strip dries before the roller reaches it, a lap mark forms at the join. Working efficiently, cutting in one section and rolling it before the edge dries, avoids the mark.
What you need
50 mm cutting-in brush.
A good-quality angled brush holds more paint and gives more control at the ceiling and skirting lines.
9-inch roller and sleeve.
A 10 to 12 mm nap sleeve gives a slight orange-peel texture that matches most existing walls. A 4 mm short-nap sleeve gives a smoother finish on flat walls.
Paint tray.
Mini roller.
For surfaces the 9-inch roller cannot reach: narrow sections between windows and doors, below window boards, behind radiators.
Loading the brush
Step 1, load and condition the brush.

Dip the brush to about one third of the bristle length. Tap the bristle tip against the inside of the paint tin (do not wipe the side) to remove excess paint from the outside of the bristles. The brush should hold paint without dripping.
Cutting in
Step 2, cut the top line.

Start at the ceiling-wall junction at the far corner from the door. Hold the brush lightly near the ferrule rather than at the end of the handle. Place the bristle tip against the ceiling surface and draw the brush along the line in one continuous stroke. The paint flows from the bristle tip to fill the angle. Work in 600 to 900 mm sections across the top of the wall.
Step 3, cut the skirting and corner lines.
Cut in along the skirting line, along window surrounds, and into the wall-to-wall corners in the same way. Work around the full perimeter of the area to be rolled before loading the roller.
Rolling
Step 4, load and condition the roller.

Pour paint into the tray to fill the deeper well section. Load the roller by rolling it into the paint and then working it back and forth on the ribbed ramp section to distribute the paint evenly around the sleeve. A fully loaded roller leaves paint with the first stroke; a poorly loaded one drags and skips.
Step 5, roll in sections.

Work in vertical strips about 600 mm wide. Apply paint with a W or M pattern stroke to distribute paint quickly, then blend with vertical passes to level the surface. Work back into the wet edge of the cut-in strip before the cut-in dries. This join should be invisible in the finished work.
Step 6, check in raking light.
Hold a lamp at a low angle to the finished wall while the paint is still wet. Misses (thin areas) show as different-sheen patches. Apply a second pass over any misses before the paint skins.
Common mistakes
Lap marks at the cut-in join.
The cut-in strip dried before the roller reached it. Cut in smaller sections and roll each section before moving along the wall.
Roller spray on ceiling and skirting.
Overloaded roller or too much speed. Use the ribbed ramp section of the tray to remove excess paint; reduce stroke speed.
Orange peel too pronounced.
Wrong nap length for the wall texture. Use a shorter nap on smooth surfaces. Allow the first coat to dry fully; the second coat flattens the texture further.
